title: Where Have All the Monkeys Gone?
author: Patrouch & ZCode
excerpt: The flying monkeys vanished for weeks. The bug wasn't in the spawn code — it was in the architecture. When your enemies only exist via a Durable Object that can't run in dev, you need a fallback.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: Where are the flying monkeys? I haven't seen them in a coon's age.

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
They're gone because of how they were architected. In Oz: Dominion, monkeys only spawn when the OzRoom Durable Object broadcasts a `monkey_wave` message over WebSocket. The DO owns wave timing so all clients spawn the same monkeys at the same positions — no desync in multiplayer.

The problem: SvelteKit's dev mode can't instantiate Durable Objects. The workerd warning we keep seeing — *"A DurableObjectNamespace in the config referenced the class 'OzRoom', but no such Durable Object class is exported from the worker"* — means the DO never starts. The WebSocket times out. No waves arrive. Zero monkeys.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: So the code was right, it just had no trigger?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Exactly. The spawn function, the monkey AI, the wing-flap animation, the rogue gold variant — all correct, all sitting idle waiting for a WebSocket message that never came. It's like writing a beautiful event handler and forgetting to attach the event listener.

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
A local fallback spawner. When the WebSocket isn't connected — `!_wsReady` — a timer fires waves locally:

- First wave 6 seconds after boot (2-3 monkeys at random positions around the player)
- Subsequent waves every 12-20 seconds
- Gold rogue monkey every 30-45 seconds (worth 15 points)
- Uses high monkey IDs (10000+) to avoid clashing with DO-assigned IDs when multiplayer comes online

The key design decision: it **goes silent automatically** once the DO connects. In production, the real DO takes over. In dev, the fallback fills the gap. The game is playable in both environments.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: Is this pattern specific to monkeys, or is it broader?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content:
It's a general principle for any game architecture where the server owns authoritative state but you still want local dev to work. Munchkins already had it — they spawn locally regardless of WebSocket state. Now monkeys match. Any future server-driven event (boss spawns, weather changes, scripted sequences) should follow the same pattern: local fallback when the server is unavailable, silent handoff when it connects.

The alternative — requiring the full backend stack to be running just to test basic gameplay — is a development friction tax you pay forever. The fallback eliminates it.

sections.title: Patrouch:
sections.content: And in production, the DO handles everything?

sections.title: ZCode:
sections.content: Right. The OzRoom DO broadcasts monkey waves to all connected clients. Wave size scales with player count — more players = more monkeys. Positions are deterministic (derived from monkeyId) so every client agrees. The local fallback is purely a dev convenience; production uses the real thing.
